answer:
**Bang-e-Dara Part 3, Chapter 26** is an untitled Urdu verse poem by Allama Iqbal (represented here by a long divider line as its "title" in the corpus metadata).[^1] It consists of 7 stanzas (153 words in English translation), evoking a nocturnal scene where the moon addresses a restless, silent wanderer in its light.[^2]

The poem opens with the moon questioning the figure's nature:

> Why in my moonlight do you wander, restless and astray?  
> Silent as a flower, like a fragrance scattered on its way.[^3]

Subsequent stanzas propose metaphors—a jeweler of night-pearls, a fish in the river of light, or a fallen star—while describing a hushed world: the lute of existence silenced, the whirlpool stilled, the town asleep.[^4] It closes by noting the paradox of the poet's heart, immune to the moon's calming spell:

> And yet the Poet's heart, a stranger still to peace,  
> How have you stayed so free of my spell's release?[^5]

Thematically, it explores restlessness amid cosmic stillness, poetic isolation, and the limits of celestial influence, with distinctive phrasing like "moonlight wander restless."[^6]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 26, [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [wiki card](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/wiki/26.card.md).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-89c390](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/#p-89c390).

[^4]: Ibid., [full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/).

[^5]: Ibid., [p-ae494e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/#p-ae494e).

[^6]: Ibid., [wiki card](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/wiki/26.card.md).
